video_title: CANDU: The Truly Modular Reactor w/ Navid Badie
gist: Navid Badie, chief nuclear engineer at Candu Energy (a division of AtkinsRéalis), walks through the engineering, safety, refurbishment and fuel-cycle features of the CANDU pressurized heavy-water reactor, framing its pressure-tube architecture, online refueling, large passive water inventories, and locally fabricable natural-uranium fuel as durable competitive moats rooted in decades of Canadian innovation and operating experience.

insights.claim: Canada's political decision to use natural uranium (uranium-235 content of about 0.73%) is the root cause from which nearly every distinctive CANDU design feature — heavy water, larger volume, pressure tubes, and online refueling — cascades.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: CANDU's entire differentiated value proposition (fuel sovereignty, isotope production, online refueling) traces to one constraint; investors evaluating CANDU should view its features as an integrated system rather than swappable options.
insights.quote: all of them I think you can tie back to the decision to use natural uranium

insights.claim: Natural uranium's low fissile content forces an extreme neutron economy where the reactor is designed to waste as few neutrons as possible and starts with very little excess reactivity, which in turn mandates online (continuous) refueling rather than periodic shutdowns.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: The neutron-economy requirement is what makes CANDU's online refueling a structural necessity rather than a convenience, underpinning its high capacity factors.
insights.quote: You design the whole reactor to waste as few neutrons as possible because you start with natural uranium.

insights.claim: Heavy water is chosen as both coolant and moderator because it is a very poor neutron absorber (low neutron loss) but a less efficient moderator than light water, requiring more of it and thus a physically larger reactor volume.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: The need for more moderator volume directly drove the pressure-tube architecture; understanding this explains why CANDU cannot simply be down-scaled like a light-water SMR.

insights.claim: CANDU adopted the pressure-tube / fuel-channel concept because the large vessel implied by heavy-water moderation, combined with Canada's lack of large pressure-vessel fabrication capability at the time, made a conventional pressure-vessel reactor infeasible.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: CANDU's modular pressure-tube design sidesteps the heavy-forging supply-chain bottleneck that constrains large PWRs/BWRs — a strategic advantage for countries without forging capacity.
insights.quote: the fabrication of large pressure vessels was a technology that was not available in Canada at the time led to the decision to uh look for an alternative to a pressure vessel reactor

insights.claim: Wall stress in a pressure boundary is proportional to the ratio of diameter to thickness, so CANDU's many small-diameter pressure tubes can be relatively thin-walled, whereas a single large heavy-water pressure vessel would require proportionately thick (and unforgeable in Canada) walls.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: The diameter-to-thickness physics is the engineering rationale for distributing the pressure boundary into hundreds of tubes — relevant when comparing CANDU manufacturability against large-vessel designs.
insights.quote: the stress inside the wall is the ratio of the diameter to the thickness

insights.claim: Each CANDU fuel channel produces a few megawatts thermal and a reactor of several hundred channels yields 1,500–3,000 MW thermal, translating to roughly 500–1,000 MW electric after the thermodynamic cycle.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Sets the output envelope for CANDU units, useful for sizing comparisons against AP1000, EPR and SMR offerings.

insights.claim: Chris Keefer frames each CANDU fuel channel as effectively a micro-reactor, with the design solving the core micro-reactor economic problem (neutron leakage, duplicated shielding/security/refueling infrastructure) by co-locating ~380–480 of them so they share neutrons and infrastructure.
insights.speaker: host (Chris Keefer)
insights.so_what: Reframes CANDU as a 'truly modular' reactor that captures modularity benefits without the diseconomies that plague standalone micro-reactors — a counter-narrative to the SMR investment thesis.
insights.quote: the can do reactor is really a fueling machine with a reactor attached to it

insights.claim: Horizontal fuel channels were chosen partly to enable refueling from both ends of the channel and to aid thermal siphoning; an early vertical pressure-tube steam-generating heavy-water (boiling) variant was built but ran only briefly due to control difficulties.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Explains why the horizontal layout is core to CANDU's refueling and passive-cooling advantages and why vertical alternatives were abandoned.

themes.theme: Online refueling as a proven moat

insights.claim: CANDU has accumulated over 1,000 reactor-years and more than 400,000 fuel-channel-years of operation, with each channel refueled once or twice a year, approaching one million online refueling operations — making online refueling a mastered, low-risk routine rather than a hazard.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: The million-operation track record neutralizes the 'opening the pressure boundary in service is dangerous' critique and supports CANDU's high availability claims.
insights.quote: we are approaching a million online refueling operation. And if you've done something a million times, this is not a concern

insights.claim: CANDU online refueling exchanges roughly four to eight fuel bundles per day on average (about one or more fuel channels per day across the fleet), with twin fueling machines pushing and receiving fuel from both ends of a channel.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the continuous-refueling cadence that lets CANDU avoid the multi-week fueling outages light-water reactors would need under natural-uranium fueling.

insights.claim: Online refueling is a moat CANDU uniquely retained: other online-refueling designs (German pebble-bed AVR/THTR, UK Magnox and advanced gas reactors) ran into difficulties or chose other options, while CANDU stuck with and perfected the fueling concept.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: CANDU's reliable online refueling is a durable differentiator that competitors abandoned — a defensible technical advantage in a field where most rivals failed at the same task.

themes.theme: Passive water inventories and accident grace time

insights.claim: A CANDU 6 / EC6 holds large nested water inventories — about 120 metric tons in the primary heat transport, ~230 tonnes of heavy-water moderator in the calandria, and ~520 tonnes of shield water — plus ~20 tonnes of high-pressure emergency cooling water and a reserve water tank larger than all of those combined by roughly a factor of two.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: These abundant, partly low-pressure water inventories act as a thermal sink that provides long accident grace times — a passive-safety selling point distinct from PWR/BWR designs.

insights.claim: Under bounding conservative assumptions, the EC6 has been shown in its generic preliminary safety report to survive a station blackout for well over 3 days without any operator intervention before steam-generator dryout, and well over a further day before onset of fuel failure.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Multi-day no-operator grace time is a marketable, regulator-relevant safety metric that could ease licensing and siting versus designs with shorter coping times.
insights.quote: it is well over 3 days with conservative assumptions uh to the onset of try out

insights.claim: Badie expects the final site-specific EC6 safety analysis, after removing conservatisms, to demonstrate well over a week before fuel failures from a station blackout, and analyses run to ~6 days with minimal operator intervention have shown no fuel damage at all.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Signals headroom in CANDU's safety case that could strengthen the licensing pitch for upcoming builds; the conservative public number understates expected performance.
insights.quote: I think you would have well over a week before uh fuel failures resulting from a station blackout

insights.claim: CANDU's passive cooling relies on thermosiphoning, with steam generators positioned higher than the reactor so that hot, less-dense primary water naturally rises to the steam generators, cools, and sinks back — sustained as long as the steam-generator secondary side is kept topped up and able to vent steam.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Gravity-driven cooling without powered pumps underpins the long station-blackout coping time and reduces reliance on active backup systems.

insights.claim: Badie characterizes CANDU as a hybrid between an active and passive reactor — engineered safety systems plus inherent passive features like huge unmoved water inventories already present in the primary circuit, calandria and shield tank.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Positions CANDU between fully-active designs (EPR) and fully-passive designs (AP1000, NuScale) — a marketing and licensing posture investors should weigh against pure-passive competitors.
insights.quote: it's it's a kind of a hybrid between an active and a and a passive reactor

insights.claim: The reserve water tank feeds multiple accident functions including a dousing system that can condense steam to rapidly drop containment pressure after a loss-of-coolant accident, and is connected to the steam-generator secondary side to extend coping time.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Multi-purpose reserve water tank is a key engineered feature enabling CANDU's extended grace times and containment pressure control.

insights.claim: Zirconium-alloy pressure tubes age under high neutron flux and reach end of life, driving CANDU's defining mid-life refurbishment in which pressure tubes, calandria tubes, spacers and feeders are all replaced to yield essentially a new core.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Refurbishment is a recurring, multi-billion-dollar capital event and a distinct CANDU business line; its scope and cadence are central to lifetime-cost analysis.

insights.claim: CANDU refurbishment occurs roughly every 30 years (some Pickering units near 40), with improved thicker/wider pressure-tube walls making ~35 years more likely, and there is active discussion of a second 're-tube' that could extend reactor life to 90–100 years.
insights.speaker: host (Chris Keefer)
insights.so_what: A potential second refurbishment extending life to a century reframes CANDU economics over very long horizons and the value of retained Darlington refurbishment know-how.

insights.claim: Canada has progressively industrialized refurbishment across generations (Pickering A large-scale fuel channel replacement after the 1983 event, through Gen 2 at Point Lepreau and Bruce 1-2, Gen 3 at Darlington and Bruce 6, now moving to Gen 3 plus), consecutively cutting the time required to refurbish each reactor.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: A learning curve that shortens refurbishment outages directly improves project economics and de-risks the upcoming pipeline of CANDU refurbishments and new builds.
insights.quote: we are consecutively reducing the time that it takes to refurbish these reactors

insights.claim: Canada has won 9 of the 41 Kroll Awards (about 22%) given by ASM for advances in zirconium metallurgy, reflecting deep proprietary understanding of in-reactor pressure-tube behavior that underpins refurbishment confidence.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Concentrated metallurgical expertise is a knowledge moat that makes CANDU pressure-tube life management hard for competitors to replicate.
insights.quote: 41 crawl awards have been given in the history of ASM and nine of them have gone to the Aclientists

insights.claim: CANDU engineers invented techniques to reposition the coil-spring spacers between pressure tubes and calandria tubes from inside the tube — magnetically for older spacer types, and via locally controlled vibration for a newer spacer style that could not be moved magnetically — to optimize spacer location and extend reactor life.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the proprietary remote-robotics tooling that lets CANDU extend life and solve in-service problems competitors cannot — part of the maintenance moat.

insights.claim: CANDU's remote-operated robotics, originally built for fueling machines, are reused to inspect pressure tubes and to visually inspect the entire calandria interior (pitch-black, highly radioactive) without contact before reinstalling new fuel channels.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Mature in-reactor robotics give CANDU a tooling and inspection capability that supports refurbishment quality and life extension.

insights.claim: CANDU reactors and CANDU-derived Indian pressurized heavy-water reactors produce about 70% of global cobalt-60, with the remaining ~30% from still-operating Chernobyl-style RBMK reactors in Russia and former Eastern Bloc countries.
insights.speaker: host (Chris Keefer)
insights.so_what: CANDU dominance in cobalt-60 (used for bulk medical and food sterilization) is a niche revenue and strategic-supply position with limited substitutes outside aging RBMKs.

insights.claim: Badie attributes CANDU's isotope advantage less to its neutron economy and more to physical access to the reactor internals via top viewing ports and reactivity-mechanism guide tubes in a non-high-pressure environment, letting isotopes be harvested regularly rather than only during refueling outages as in other designs.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Continuous core access reframes CANDU as a flexible isotope-production platform (cobalt-60, molybdenum, lutetium), an expanding revenue stream distinct from electricity sales.
insights.quote: the the thing that I think is a differentiator for us is the fact that we have access to the reactor uh internals

insights.claim: Because CANDU uses natural uranium, its simple ~half-meter, 37-element fuel bundle can be fabricated locally under license without the highly complex enrichment and precision manufacturing required for PWR/BWR fuel, and CANDU operators in Korea, China, Argentina and Romania already make their own fuel.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Local fuel fabrication gives importing countries fuel-supply sovereignty and removes dependence on a few Western fuel vendors — a geopolitical selling point that differentiates CANDU exports.
insights.quote: because it uses natural uranium um this can be produced anywhere

insights.claim: CANDU offers buyer fuel sovereignty even for countries that do not mine uranium, because fuel fabrication — a major value-add step in the PWR/BWR fuel cycle dominated by vendors like GE and Westinghouse — can be domesticated for CANDU's simple bundle.
insights.speaker: host (Chris Keefer)
insights.so_what: Fuel-sovereignty is a recurring reason cited for CANDU imports; it reduces long-term dependence on Western enrichment/fabrication supply chains and is a key export differentiator.

insights.claim: The CANDU fuel pencil's sheath collapses onto the uranium-dioxide pellet under pressurized-water flow, holding the pellet tight to improve heat transfer into the coolant — a deliberate design feature contributing to fuel durability.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Illustrates the engineered robustness of the simple, locally-fabricable CANDU bundle, supporting the case that low complexity does not mean low performance.

insights.claim: Keefer contrasts the EPR's conservative active-safety paradigm (four 100% trains, many diesel backups) — which produced an unwieldy, expensive, hard-to-build plant whose cost and schedule overruns were worsened by divergent European regulator requirements — with CANDU's hybrid approach.
insights.speaker: host (Chris Keefer)
insights.so_what: Frames CANDU's hybrid safety and modular construction as avoiding the overbuild that drove EPR cost overruns — relevant to buildability and cost-overrun risk in nuclear investment.

insights.claim: Keefer likens CANDU's shield-tank and calandria arrangement to NuScale's reactor-in-a-swimming-pool concept, noting the CANDU core effectively sits inside a built-in pool of shield water — an inherent-safety feature predating modern SMR marketing.
insights.speaker: host (Chris Keefer)
insights.so_what: Suggests CANDU embodies passive-pool safety features that newer SMRs market as novel, a talking point for CANDU's relative maturity.

insights.claim: AtkinsRéalis's Candu Energy is targeting near-term new build, expecting to complete Cernavoda units 3 and 4 in Romania with the extended-grace-time steam-generator connection and to soon secure a site for the EC6 design.
insights.speaker: Navid Badie
insights.so_what: Names concrete near-term CANDU project pipeline (Cernavoda 3 and 4, EC6 siting) that investors tracking AtkinsRéalis can monitor as catalysts.
